Eigenvalues
The scalar λ is called an eigenvalue of a square n × n matrix A if there exists a nonzero column vector u that satisfies
Au = λ u

(B.4.1)

When λ is an eigenvalue, any u ≠ 0 satisfying Eq. (B.4.1) is called an eigenvector.
If for a specific value of λ, det(λΙ – A) ≠ 0, then the linear system (B.4.1) has only the trivial
solution u = 0, hence λ is not an eigenvalue. On the other hand, if det(λΙ – A) vanishes, then
(B.4.1) admits a nonzero solution. The eigenvalues are therefore the roots of the equation:
det(λI –A) = 0

(B.4.2)

The polynomial det(λΙ – A) is called the characteristic polynomial of the n × n matrix A
and Eq. (B.4.2) is called the characteristic equation of the matrix A. The characteristic
­polynomial is an n-th-degree polynomial in λ, with the coefficient of λn equal to 1 and with
its n roots equal to the n eigenvalues λ1, λ2, …, λn of the matrix A. The characteristic polynomial can therefore be written in the form

Basic Matrix Theory

det ( λ I − A) = ( λ − λ1 )( λ − λ2 )  ( λ − λn )

751

(B.4.3)

This equation implies det ( − A) = ( − λ1 ) ( − λ2 ) ( − λ n ) = ( −1) λ1λ2  λ n or det ( A) = λ1λ2  λn,
i.e. the determinant of an n × n matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues. Therefore, a
square matrix is invertible if and only if all its eigenvalues are nonzero.
Other important properties of the eigenvalues include the following.
n

•
•
•

The eigenvalues of a diagonal or triangular matrix are equal to its diagonal elements aii.
The eigenvalues of the transpose AT are the same as the eigenvalues of A.
If λi, i = 1, …, n are the eigenvalues of a matrix A, then λip are the eigenvalues of the
p-th power Ap, and e λi are the eigenvalues of the exponential eA.
For a square 2 × 2 matrix A, the characteristic polynomial is

  1 0   a

= ( λ − 1)( λ − 2 ) − ( −4 )( −3) = λ 2 − 3λ − 10

(B.4.5)

752

Appendix B

We could have obtained the same result by using Eq. (B.4.4). Note that det(A) = –10. The characteristic equation is

λ 2 − 3λ − 10 = 0 ⇒ ( λ − 5 )( λ + 2 ) = 0
and the eigenvalues are λ1 = 5 and λ2 = –2.

eigenvalue). Because of the presence of the zero eigenvalue, we can immediately conclude that
the matrix A is singular (does not have an inverse).

B.5

The Cayley–Hamilton Theorem and the Resolvent Identity
The Cayley–Hamilton theorem states the following: Every square n × n matrix A satisfies
its characteristic equation. In other words, if the characteristic equation of a square n × n
matrix A is

λ n + a1λ n −1 +  + an −1λ + an = 0

(B.5.1)

then the following holds true
An + a1An −1 +  + an −1A + an I = 0

(B.5.2)

Basic Matrix Theory

753

The Cayley–Hamilton theorem is useful in calculating the inverse of a matrix, if its characteristic polynomial has already been calculated. Indeed, multiplying Eq. (B.5.2) by A–1, we
obtain An −1 + a1An − 2 +  + an −1I + an A−1 = 0 , hence
1
A−1 = − ( An −1 + a1An − 2 +  + an −1I )
(B.5.3)
an
Another important byproduct of the Cayley–Hamilton theorem is the resolvent identity.
The matrix (λI – A)–1 is called the resolvent matrix, and plays a very important role in the
analysis of dynamic systems. The Cayley–Hamilton theorem leads to an explicit formula for
the resolvent matrix. In particular, if the characteristic polynomial of a square n × n matrix
A has already been calculated and is equal to λ n + a1λ n −1 +  + an −1λ + an , then the adjugate
of (λI – A) is given by the formula:
Adj ( λ I − A) = I λ n −1 + ( A + a1I ) λ n − 2 +  + ( An −1 + a1An − 2 +  + an − 2 A + an −1I )

(B.5.4)

hence the resolvent matrix can be expressed as
( λ I − A)−1 =
=

Adj ( λ I − A)
det ( λ I − A)

I λ n −1 + ( A + a1I ) λ n − 2 +  + ( An −1 + a1An − 2 +  + an − 2 A + an −1I )

(B.5.5)

λ n + a1λ n −1 + a2 λ n − 2 +  + an −1λ + an

The last equation is called the resolvent identity.

B.7.2

Matrix Calculations in Maple
The basic operations of addition, subtraction and multiplication are performed in Maple
as A+B, A–B and A.B, respectively. Further matrix operations and calculations may be
performed using the LinearAlgebra package of Maple. Table B.2 summarizes the main
matrix operations in Maple.
Calculations in Example B.1 can be performed with Maple as follows:
> with(LinearAlgebra):
> A:=Matrix([[1,2],[0,3]]):
> B:=Matrix([[4,0],[2,2]]):
> C:=Matrix([[7,0,0],[0,8,9]]):
